text: Measurement of gas-phase organic compunds at FLAME-4 using GC×GC-ToFMS Abstract: There is currently limited understanding of secondary organic aerosol (SOA) processes occurring within biomass burning (BB) plumes largely due to a lack of knowledge regarding the primary non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) released by wildland fires. In this study, the gas-phase organic compounds collected on sorbent cartridges during laboratory burns were analyzed using two-dimensional gas chromatography–time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC × GC–ToFMS). This GC × GC–ToFMS approach provided the most extensive data set of BB NMOCs to date with identifications of 708 positively or tentatively identified compounds using peak selection criteria. Emission factors (EFs) for these compounds are estimated and comparisons made among burns of six different vegetative fuels including: conifer branches, grasses, agricultural residue, peat and shrubs. The number of identified compounds per burn ranged from 129 to 474, with a large number of isomers identified in the emissions. For example, 38 different monoterpene isomers were identified in the coniferous fuel burns and these isomer ratios align with literature values of essential oils, suggesting that these compositions may be useful in modelling fuel-dependent terpenes emissions. Also, 11 sesquiterpenes were identified and characterized in the gas-phase BB emissions for the first time. The EFs for all identified compounds are provided and compared to explore the possible contributions of these compounds to SOA formation within BB plumes.
explanation: The text is highly domain-specific with plausible measurements and terminology typical of a real abstract; a minor typographical error doesn't strongly indicate AI authorship.
